issues.description: All four endpoints (GET, PUT, DELETE, POST) on the report outputs controller have no authentication guard. The JSDoc explicitly marks '@x-autobe-authorization-type null' and '@x-autobe-authorization-actor null', meaning no MemberAuth or equivalent guard is applied. These endpoints handle sensitive ERP report data and allow unauthenticated creation, modification, and deletion of report output records.
issues.suggestion: Apply a NestJS Guard (e.g., @MemberAuth() decorator or equivalent AuthGuard) to all endpoints in this controller. Report data is sensitive business data that must require authentication at minimum.

issues.description: The metrics update endpoint (PATCH /reportOutputs/:reportOutputId/metrics) has no authentication guard (@x-autobe-authorization-type null, @x-autobe-authorization-actor null). Any unauthenticated caller can modify metric breakdown values for any report output. The spec mentions 'report:view' permission check but this is delegated entirely to the provider with no controller-level guard.
issues.suggestion: Apply authentication guard at the controller level. Do not rely solely on provider-level authorization checks without a controller-level guard to reject unauthenticated requests early.

issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling. Authentication endpoints (join, login, refresh) in particular should use structured logging to capture security-relevant events. Login failures are not logged with sufficient context (IP, timestamp, attempted email) for security monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Replace console.log with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston). For auth endpoints, log failed authentication attempts with IP address and timestamp (but never log passwords or tokens).

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to authentication endpoints (join, login, refresh). These endpoints are susceptible to brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting middleware (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to authentication endpoints, especially login and refresh.

issues.description: Organization scoping check compares `erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id` against `props.member.session_id` instead of the member's organization ID. This means the authorization check is effectively broken — a lock belonging to any organization will fail the check (since session_id is not an organization_id), or an attacker could potentially access locks by manipulating session context. The guard never correctly validates organization ownership.
issues.suggestion: Compare `lock.timesheet.erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id` against the member's actual organization ID (e.g., `props.member.organization_id` or resolved from session), not `props.member.session_id`.

issues.description: This provider is a stub that returns the raw update payload directly as the response without performing any database operation, authorization check, or organization scoping. Any authenticated member can call this endpoint and receive back whatever they submitted, with no access control enforced.
issues.suggestion: Implement the actual database update with proper authorization checks: verify the lock belongs to the caller's organization, verify the caller owns the lock (locked_by_user_id), and persist changes transactionally.

issues.description: The delete organization provider is a completely empty function — it performs no authorization check, no ownership validation, no database operation, and returns void silently. Any authenticated member can call this endpoint with any organizationId and receive a success response.
issues.suggestion: Implement authorization checks (verify caller has admin/owner role in the organization), verify the organization exists, and perform the actual soft-delete or hard-delete with proper cascading.

issues.description: This provider creates report definition filters without any authentication or organization scoping check. It only verifies the report definition exists by ID but does not verify the caller has permission to modify it or that it belongs to the caller's organization. Any unauthenticated or unauthorized caller could add filters to any report definition.
issues.suggestion: Add organization scoping: resolve the caller's selected organization, verify the report definition belongs to that organization, and enforce role-based permission before creating filters.

issues.description: This provider returns the input body cast directly as the return type without any database persistence, authorization check, or organization scoping. It is a stub that accepts any input and echoes it back, bypassing all business logic and security controls.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper authorization (verify caller has permission to create report definitions), resolve organization context, persist to database, and return the created entity.

issues.description: This provider retrieves a report generation run by ID without any authentication or organization scoping check. Any caller who knows a valid UUID can retrieve any report generation run from any organization, exposing cross-tenant data.
issues.suggestion: Resolve the caller's organization context, add a WHERE clause filtering by organization ID, and verify the caller has permission to view report generation runs.

issues.description: The organization context is resolved via `MyGlobal.getSelectedOrganizationId?.()` — a global static method — rather than from the authenticated member's session/token. This pattern is unsafe in a multi-tenant concurrent environment: the 'selected organization' could be shared state or improperly scoped, allowing one user's request to operate in another user's organization context.
issues.suggestion: Resolve the organization context from the authenticated member's JWT/session payload passed as a parameter, not from a global static method. Pass the member payload to this provider and extract organization_id from it.

issues.description: The organization ID used for department creation is obtained via `(props as any)?.member?.organization_id` — an unsafe cast that may resolve to undefined. Additionally, the uniqueness check for department name is performed globally (not scoped to the organization), meaning a department name unique within one organization could be incorrectly rejected due to a name existing in another organization.
issues.suggestion: Properly resolve the organization ID from the authenticated member's session. Scope the uniqueness check to the specific organization: `WHERE name = ? AND organization_id = ? AND deleted_at IS NULL`.

issues.description: The organization ID for authorization is derived from the project record itself (not from the authenticated member's session), meaning there is no cross-check that the authenticated member belongs to the project's organization. A member from organization A could update a project in organization B if they know the project UUID.
issues.suggestion: Resolve the organization ID from the authenticated member's session/payload and verify it matches the project's organization ID before allowing the update.

issues.description: This provider returns an empty hardcoded result with no database query, no authorization check, and no organization scoping. Any member can call this endpoint for any projectId and receive an empty success response.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper authorization, verify the project belongs to the member's organization, and execute the actual paginated database query.

issues.description: The provider does not verify that the authenticated member has permission to manage project memberships (e.g., project manager or admin role). Any authenticated member can add any employee to any project they can access. Additionally, the `nowDate` is hardcoded to a fixed timestamp ('2026-03-18T12:39:46.688Z') instead of using `new Date()`, which will cause incorrect audit timestamps.
issues.suggestion: Add role/permission check before allowing membership creation. Replace the hardcoded `nowDate` with `new Date()` for accurate timestamps.

issues.description: Activity log entries are typically immutable audit records. Allowing members to update their own activity log entries (action_type, target_entity_type, summary, details, occurred_at) undermines audit integrity. The `updated_at` field is set to `body.occurred_at` (user-supplied) rather than the current server time, allowing backdating.
issues.suggestion: Activity log entries should be immutable. If updates are required, restrict them to non-audit fields only. Set `updated_at` to `new Date()` (server time), not the user-supplied `occurred_at`.

issues.description: The organization ID is obtained via `(props.member as any).organization_id` — an unsafe cast that bypasses TypeScript type safety. If `organization_id` is not present on `MemberPayload`, this will be `undefined`, causing the query to return data across all organizations (no tenant isolation).
issues.suggestion: Ensure `organization_id` is a typed field on `MemberPayload`. If it requires session lookup, perform the database query to resolve it. Never use `as any` for security-critical tenant scoping.

issues.description: The versioning lock update provider is a non-functional stub that returns the request body as the response. This means concurrent edit protection (versioning locks) is completely non-functional — the lock system that prevents concurrent timesheet edits does not work.
issues.suggestion: Implement the actual lock update logic with database persistence, ownership verification (locked_by_user_id), and organization scoping.

issues.description: All controller methods use `console.log(error)` for error handling, which may expose stack traces and internal details in logs without structured context. This is the standard AutoBE pattern.
issues.suggestion: Consider using a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger) with log levels and request context for better observability and to avoid leaking sensitive information in production logs.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to timesheet workflow endpoints (submit, reject, create). High-frequency calls could be used to abuse the workflow system.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the API gateway or using NestJS ThrottlerModule, especially on workflow transition endpoints.

issues.description: The contract is fetched by contractId without verifying that it belongs to the authenticated member. Any authenticated member can retrieve any contract by guessing or knowing a valid UUID, leading to cross-user data exposure.
issues.suggestion: Add a where clause that ties the contract to the member's organization or employee ID, e.g., `where: { id: props.contractId, erp_hrm_time_tracking_member_id: member.id }`, and throw a 403/404 if the record does not belong to the requesting member.

issues.description: This provider accepts no member/session context, meaning there is no ownership or authorization check before updating a report definition filter. Any caller who can reach this endpoint can modify any filter for any report definition.
issues.suggestion: Accept a `member: MemberPayload` parameter and verify that the report definition belongs to the member's organization before performing the update.

issues.description: The employee lookup only checks `deleted_at: null` but does not verify that the target employee belongs to the same organization as the requesting member. A member from one organization could create timesheets for employees in another organization.
issues.suggestion: Add `erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id: organizationId` to the employee lookup where clause to ensure cross-organization timesheet creation is prevented.

issues.description: The organization ID is derived by casting the session object with `as unknown as { erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id: string }`. If the session record does not actually contain this field (e.g., schema mismatch), the value will be `undefined` and silently used as such in subsequent queries, potentially bypassing organization scoping.
issues.suggestion: Select the field explicitly in the Prisma query (`select: { erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id: true }`) and validate that the returned value is a non-empty string before proceeding.

issues.description: No audit logging is performed when a report definition filter is updated. Changes to report filters could affect business-critical reporting and should be traceable.
issues.suggestion: Log update operations (who changed what, when) to an audit log or structured logging system.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/172 files] [3 chunks, 28→25 issues] The most critical finding is that the report output endpoints (CRUD and metrics) have no authentication guards whatsoever, allowing unauthenticated access to sensitive ERP business data. This is a significant broken access control vulnerability. The member-facing endpoints (contracts, projects, activity logs, timer sessions) correctly use @MemberAuth() guards. The auth controller properly handles join/login/refresh flows but lacks rate limiting. Organization scoping and permission enforcement are delegated entirely to provider functions with no controller-level defense-in-depth. Overall security posture is poor due to the unprotected report output endpoints.

issues.description: Function returns a '501 Not implemented' error - entire implementation is just a stub.
issues.suggestion: Implement actual update logic based on business requirements.

issues.description: Function returns empty Promise<void> with no implementation - critical missing functionality.
issues.suggestion: Implement timelog update logic including validation and business rules.

issues.description: Function returns '501 Not implemented' - TODO comment indicates placeholder.
issues.suggestion: Replace TODO with proper contract update implementation.

issues.description: Function returns null as unknown type - no actual creation logic.
issues.suggestion: Implement report generation run creation with validation and proper data insertion.

issues.description: Function creates a placeholder object with date normalization but no database persistence - returns normalized input as output.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper database insertion with validation and return actual created record.

issues.description: Function returns a date string as unknown type - placeholder implementation with comment.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper organization update logic with authorization checks.

issues.description: Activity log update allows user to modify 'occurred_at' and 'updated_at' to arbitrary values - unrealistic for audit logs.
issues.suggestion: Activity logs should be immutable or only allow limited updates (like summaries). Remove ability to modify timestamps.

issues.description: Organization ID inferred from project, not from member session - could lead to cross-organization access.
issues.suggestion: Verify member belongs to the same organization as the project before allowing update.

issues.description: Requires caller membership role to be 'project-lead' - other roles (like 'admin') might also need update permissions.
issues.suggestion: Check requirements document for proper role-based authorization rules.
